title: Spark Intro
content:
Apache Spark Introduction

The Unified Analytics Engine

Apache Spark is a multi-language engine for executing data engineering, data science, and machine learning on single-node machines or clusters.



  • Speed: Run workloads 100x faster than Hadoop MapReduce.
  • Ease of Use: Write applications quickly in Java, Scala, Python, R, and SQL.
  • Generality: Combine SQL, streaming, and complex analytics.

title: Getting Started
content:
Getting Started with PySpark

Initializing Spark

To start a Spark application in Python (PySpark), you first need to create a SparkSession, which is the entry point to programming Spark with the Dataset and DataFrame API.

SparkSession
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# Initialize SparkSession
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("My First Spark App") \
    .getOrCreate()

print("Spark Version:", spark.version)

title: Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs)
content:
Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs)

The Core Abstraction

RDDs are a fault-tolerant collection of elements that can be operated on in parallel. They are the fundamental data structure of Apache Spark.

Creating an RDD
# Create an RDD from a list
data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
rdd = spark.sparkContext.parallelize(data)

# Perform a transformation and an action
squared_rdd = rdd.map(lambda x: x * x)
print("Squared elements:", squared_rdd.collect())

title: DataFrames
content:
Spark DataFrames

Structured Data Processing

A DataFrame is a Dataset organized into named columns. It is conceptually equivalent to a table in a relational database or a data frame in R/Python (Pandas), but with richer optimizations under the hood.

Creating DataFrames
data = [("Alice", 25), ("Bob", 30), ("Charlie", 35)]
columns = ["Name", "Age"]

# Create DataFrame
df = spark.createDataFrame(data, columns)

# Display the data
df.show()

title: DataFrame Operations
content:
DataFrame Operations

Transforming Data

DataFrames provide a domain-specific language for structured data manipulation. You can filter, select, group, and join data efficiently.

Filtering and Selecting
df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ("Alice", 25), ("Bob", 30), ("Charlie", 35)
], ["Name", "Age"])

# Select an older age group
older_df = df.filter(df["Age"] > 28)

# Show only the names
older_df.select("Name").show()

title: Spark SQL
content:
Spark SQL

Querying with SQL

Spark SQL is a Spark module for structured data processing. It allows you to query DataFrames using standard SQL syntax.

SQL Queries on DataFrames
df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ("Alice", 25), ("Bob", 30), ("Charlie", 35)
], ["Name", "Age"])

# Register the DataFrame as a SQL temporary view
df.createOrReplaceTempView("people")

# Query the view
sqlDF = spark.sql("SELECT * FROM people WHERE Age > 28")
sqlDF.show()

title: Spark MLlib
content:
Spark MLlib

Machine Learning Library

MLlib is Spark's scalable machine learning library. It provides high-quality algorithms for classification, regression, clustering, and collaborative filtering.

Linear Regression Example
from pyspark.ml.regression import LinearRegression
from pyspark.ml.linalg import Vectors

# Simple dataset of (label, features)
data = [(1.0, Vectors.dense([1.0, 0.1, -1.0])),
        (0.0, Vectors.dense([2.0, 1.1, 1.0])),
        (1.0, Vectors.dense([1.0, 0.1, -1.0]))]

df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ["label", "features"])

# Train a model
lr = LinearRegression(maxIter=10, regParam=0.3, elasticNetParam=0.8)
model = lr.fit(df)

print("Coefficients:", model.coefficients)

title: Spark Streaming
content:
Spark Streaming

Real-time Data Processing

Spark Streaming extends the core Spark API to enable scalable, high-throughput processing of live data streams from sources like Kafka, Flume, and Kinesis.

Structured Streaming Concept
# This is a conceptual example for Structured Streaming
print("Configuring Streaming source...")
# lines = spark.readStream.format("socket").option("host", "localhost").option("port", 9999).load()
# ...
print("Ready to stream real-time events!")

title: Spark Architecture
content:
Spark Architecture

Under the Hood

Spark uses a master-slave architecture. The Driver Program distributes tasks to Worker Nodes (executors) across the cluster using a Cluster Manager (like YARN, Mesos, or Kubernetes).

Architecture Concept
# The SparkContext connects to the Cluster Manager
# which allocates resources across the nodes.
print("Driver Program ⟷ Cluster Manager ⟷ Worker Nodes")
print("Your code runs on the Executors (Nodes).")

title: Transformations vs Actions
content:
Transformations vs Actions

Lazy Evaluation

Transformations (like `map`, `filter`) create a new RDD/DataFrame but are lazy—they don't execute immediately. Actions (like `collect`, `count`) trigger the actual computation across the cluster.

Lazy Evaluation
rdd = spark.sparkContext.parallelize([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])

# Transformation (Nothing happens yet)
mapped = rdd.map(lambda x: x * 2)

# Action (Spark finally does the work)
result = mapped.collect()
print("Result after Action:", result)

title: Key-Value Pairs
content:
RDD Key-Value Pairs

Working with Pairs

Spark excels at operations on distributed pairs. Pair RDDs expose special operations like reduceByKey and groupByKey to aggregate data distributed across nodes.

reduceByKey Example
data = [("a", 1), ("b", 1), ("a", 1)]
pair_rdd = spark.sparkContext.parallelize(data)

# Aggregate values by their keys
reduced = pair_rdd.reduceByKey(lambda x, y: x + y)
print("Word counts:", reduced.collect())

title: Caching & Persistence
content:
Caching and Persistence

Optimizing Reuse

When you use the same dataset multiple times, you should cache it in memory. This prevents Spark from re-evaluating the entire dependency graph from scratch.

Caching DataFrames
df = spark.createDataFrame([("A", 1), ("B", 2)], ["Letter", "Num"])

# Cache in memory
df.cache()

# First action computes and caches the data
print("Count:", df.count())

# Subsequent actions are drastically faster!
print("Count again:", df.count())

title: Broadcast Variables
content:
Broadcast Variables

Read-Only Distribution

A broadcast variable allows you to keep a read-only variable cached on each machine rather than shipping a copy of it with tasks. Used for small, universally needed lookup tables.

Broadcasting Data
lookup_table = {"NY": "New York", "CA": "California"}
broadcast_var = spark.sparkContext.broadcast(lookup_table)

data = ["NY", "CA", "NY"]
rdd = spark.sparkContext.parallelize(data)

# Use the broadcast variable inside the workers
mapped = rdd.map(lambda x: broadcast_var.value[x])
print("Expanded states:", mapped.collect())

title: Accumulators
content:
Accumulators

Distributed Counters

Accumulators are variables that can only be "added" to through associative operations. They are used to implement counters or sums efficiently across a distributed cluster.

Counting Across Nodes
# Create an accumulator variable
accum = spark.sparkContext.accumulator(0)

def count_evens(x):
    global accum
    if x % 2 == 0:
        accum += 1

rdd = spark.sparkContext.parallelize([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
# Accumulate during an action
rdd.foreach(lambda x: count_evens(x))

print("Total even numbers:", accum.value)

title: Partitioning & Shuffling
content:
Partitioning & Shuffling

Data Distribution

Spark divides data into partitions. Certain operations (like joins or groupBys) require moving data across nodes to arrange it by key, which is called a Shuffle. Shuffling is expensive and a major target for optimization.

Checking Partitions
rdd = spark.sparkContext.parallelize([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 4) # Request 4 partitions
print("Partitions:", rdd.getNumPartitions())

# Repartitioning shuffles data over the network
re_rdd = rdd.repartition(6)
print("New Partitions:", re_rdd.getNumPartitions())

title: Spark SQL Joins
content:
Spark SQL Joins

Combining DataFrames

You can heavily utilize standard join strategies (inner, outer, left, right) on DataFrames. Spark optimizes these natively using Hash Joins, Sort Merge Joins, and Broadcast Joins.

Joining Example
emps = spark.createDataFrame([(1, "Alice", 100), (2, "Bob", 200)], ["id", "name", "dept_id"])
depts = spark.createDataFrame([(100, "HR"), (200, "Engineering")], ["dept_id", "dept_name"])

# Left outer join
joined_df = emps.join(depts, on="dept_id", how="left")
joined_df.show()

title: User-Defined Functions
content:
User-Defined Functions (UDFs)

Custom SQL Logistics

When built-in SQL functions aren't enough, you can create UDFs. These allow you to apply custom Python logic directly against columns in your DataFrame.

Using UDFs
from pyspark.sql.functions import udf
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType

# Define the Python function
def make_loud(text):
    return text.upper() + "!!!"

# Register it as a UDF
loud_udf = udf(make_loud, StringType())

df = spark.createDataFrame([("hello",), ("world",)], ["word"])
df.withColumn("yelled", loud_udf(df["word"])).show()

title: Performance Tuning
content:
Spark Performance Tuning

Mastering the Cluster

Tuning a Spark application requires addressing data skew, minimizing network shuffles, leveraging the Catalyst Optimizer, tuning Executor memory/cores, and correctly employing Broadcast Joins.

  • Avoid Data Skew: Balance your partitions so no single node does all the work.
  • Minimize Shuffles: Avoid wide transformations (like `groupByKey`) where possible.
  • Tweak Memory: Use options like `spark.executor.memory` appropriately.
  • Broadcast Small Tables: Force a Broadcast Join to prevent shuffling large amounts of data.
